data.title: Super Micro Computer: Why It's Still Not Too Late To Buy
data.description:
Wow, the volatility in Super Micro Computer, Inc. stock (NASDAQ:SMCI) (NEOE:SMCI:CA) is quite stunning even though I consider myself a growth investor who has enough experience in dealing with highly volatile stocks. I believe even the most experienced price action investors may have found it challenging in navigating the roller coaster ride that we have had to do deal with over the past two weeks on SMCI.

For those of you who have been following Super Micro's developments, I believe you will have felt assured as Super Micro has finally "closed" the non-compliance issue with its Nasdaq listing requirements. As a result, I believe we have moved past the most daunting challenges that could stymie an improvement in investor confidence, even though I think it's apt to argue that SMCI may not be totally out of the woods yet. Here's why.

If you have taken a closer look at SMCI's delayed filings that it delivered this week, the company listed out several risk factors that require further scrutiny in order for us to assess whether it could impact SMCI’s competitiveness relative to its arch rivals. Well, the company presented an exhaustive list, which I believe enunciate the crucial elements in its thesis that investors are highly encouraged to pay close attention to.

Investors should consider whether the company could be subject to further regulatory scrutiny, given the weaknesses highlighted in its internal controls. And not just that, Super Micro may also face further impediments in accessing capital markets to fund its R&D and scaling up its manufacturing capacity. For a tech hardware company that has demonstrated difficulty in maintaining consistency in its profitability metrics, I don't think it is an insignificant matter for investors to understand. In other words, we need to give more time to assess how the company will "remediate the material weaknesses and deficiencies," which could hamper a more aggressive valuation re-rating over the next year.

Despite that, I can comprehend if investors believe that the worst is likely over for SMCI, which is consistent with my previous article, I indicated that the market would likely focus its attention on how well Blackwell will ramp through 2026. While management presented an aggressive outlook to regain investor confidence, it seems like the market has discounted the execution risks attributed to the company’s ability to scale effectively while overcoming potential liquidity challenges. And weaknesses over the state of Super Micro's internal control systems cannot be ignored, as the company flagged it in its risks disclosures on its delayed 10K following, including the following:

We have concluded that our internal control over financial reporting was not effective as of June 30, 2024 due to the existence of material weaknesses in such controls, and we have also concluded that our disclosure controls and procedures were not effective as of June 30, 2024 due to material weaknesses in our internal control over financial reporting, all as described in Part II, Item 9A, “Controls and Procedures” of this Annual Report.” - (Supermicro 10-K)

I believe this is not a trivial matter, as Super Micro faces a more intensive competitive landscape against rivals such as Dell (DELL) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE). Therefore, I'm not at all surprised that the stock fell back into a bear market after topping out at the $66.5 level last week, as profit-taking preceded its delayed filings. Hence, there is a possibility that the downside bias may persist through March, although the specter of revisiting the lows that SMCI struck in February seems unlikely, given that the company has already filed its delayed regulatory requirements with Nasdaq and maintained its listing requirements.

To be clear, I'm not trying to say that the additional risks indicated in the company's recent report are "infinitesimal," or immaterial when evaluating its bullish thesis. What I'm pointing out is that the market has likely taken into account the execution risks. Of course, it may be too early for us to ascertain whether additional regulatory scrutiny or heightened investor pessimism could worsen Super Micro’s ability to compete effectively with its peers. In addition, we may also not be fully in the picture on whether the financing risks for Super Micro could significantly affect its ability to scale, and thus, placing its 2026 outlook at significant risks.

As a reminder, the company projected $40 billion in revenue by 2026. However, the updated Wall Street estimates suggests analysts expect only $33.1 billion, which is more than 17% below what the company has presented. Therefore, I think it's quite clear that analysts have not fully bought into SMCI's projections yet. Now, here's the thing about these estimates. Since it's still quite early following the company's delayed filings, I believe Wall Street will likely accord some time for the company to prove that it can remediate the weaknesses in its internal controls. Therefore, analysts are likely to err on the side of caution in the near term, and this is clearly shown by the majority of Hold ratings on SMCI by Wall Street, corroborating the uncertainty in its proposition. However, bulls could also argue that Wall Street's increased caution could also lower the bar for underperformance, particularly since analysts have yet to upgrade their estimates in accordance to the company’s projections (in fact far from it). Which means that SMCI could outperform over the next few quarters, while the market takes its time to assess whether to give the company the benefit of the doubt, while looking past its recent compliance challenges.

Therefore, the main issue that I believe investors are struggling with Super Micro's bullish thesis is probably linked to its inconsistent profitability metrics over the past few years. As we can glean from above, SMCI’s free cash flow margin estimates are expected to hover just above the 0% mark, which is a significant downgrade from the highs in 2023. Hence, with the restraints being imposed on the company in terms of probably less competitive financing terms or even impediments in raising funds to scale up capacity, this could affect investor sentiments even as Super Micro scales its Blackwell production.

We already know from Nvidia’s earnings conference that near time gross margins are expected to be less robust at this early stage of scaling up production. From Dell’s earnings release this week, we also know that even Super Micro's closest rivals are facing challenges with profitability in ramping up Blackwell. However, Dell also has more profitable enterprise segments that help to mitigate the lower margins profile of high-performance AI servers. In contrast, Super Micro's more concentrated hyperscaler exposure could further affect its ability to adjust pricing and compete to gain market share. Therefore, the previous advantages with Hopper architecture are assessed to be less pronounced for the current Blackwell generation currently. Hence, I believe the market may take at least two or three quarters to analyze the profitability profile of the current ramp before revaluing SMCI's well-battered valuations.

There is little doubt that the market had de-rated SMCI’s valuation multiples from the highest it achieved in March last year. However, back then, the multiple of more than 43 times forward earnings was likely unjustified and demonstrated to be so, given the setbacks and execution risks emanating from Super Micro's regulatory challenges as well as more competitive headwinds attributed to its rivals.

Based on SMCI's current forward multiple of 14 times earnings, it's just slightly above industry median of about 12.7 times. In other words, the premium multiple has been deflated, which is arguably justified. For a moment last week, SMCI's multiple reached above 23 times earnings before the recent pullback that saw the stock fall back again to a bear Market. So the question is whether the market has considered the extent of the weaknesses as reported by the company in its disclosures, or should we be more circumspect in anticipating further downside possibilities moving from here.

Well, the good news is the price action for SMCI seems constructive, suggesting the market is likely more confident of its recovery and doesn't expect the stock to fall back to the lows we saw back in February or even November last year.

There was a bull trap that occurred last week, as sellers likely took profit, anticipating challenges in its risks disclosure. That has proved astute, although the extent of the pullback suggests we should be close to a consolidation zone somewhere just above the $40 level, in my opinion. However, further downside bias could still occur, given the volatility seen in tech stocks this week. Therefore, investors should consider buying into SMCI progressively to lower their risks of catching the falling knife.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: The More They Drop, The More I Buy (Up To 11% Yields)
data.description:
One of the beauties of being a dividend investor is that you actually cheer whenever the market beats down the stock price of companies that you own. This is because, as a dividend investor, I am focused primarily on the income that my investments generate for me. The performance of the stock price in the near term means little, and in fact, it benefits me when it drops because then I can buy more shares of the company at a higher yield, thereby increasing my passive income stream even faster.

On top of that, if the company is buying back stock, it can repurchase shares at a lower price, further reducing its payout ratio and increasing its ability to grow the dividend over time.

With that in mind, here are four dividend investments that have pulled back meaningfully recently and that, I believe, make for attractive buys right now.

Buy The Dip Opportunity #1

The first one we will discuss is the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

While I do not own it myself because I have been able to outperform SCHD from a total return, dividend yield, and dividend growth perspective by selecting my own individual stocks (as I discussed in a recent article), I still think SCHD is a very attractive, diversified dividend growth investment for investors who want to take a more passive approach to investing.

As the chart below shows, SCHD has dropped by almost 5% since late November, and I believe this dip is a buying opportunity.

The market has been selling off high-yield stocks in general recently as interest rates are expected to remain higher for longer due to persistent inflation and the potential inflationary impact of Trump's pro-tariff policy bent.

That said, SCHD owns some of the highest-quality dividend growth stocks, and its yield is now well above 3.5%, and its dividend growth rate has been a double-digit CAGR over the past decade, five years, and 12 months, making it a highly compelling combination of dividend yield and dividend growth for risk-averse, more passive investors. Of course, if inflation remains higher for longer and long-term interest rates continue to rise, SCHD will likely not see much in the way of appreciation for a while. However, given the quality of its underlying holdings and the consistency of their dividend growth, we still expect SCHD to continue growing its payout at a strong clip for years to come. As a result, for investors who have a reasonably long-time horizon and who are primarily focused on growing their dividend income, right now is a great buying opportunity.

Buy The Dip Opportunity #2

Another buy-the-dip opportunity is Delek Logistics Partners (DKL). It has dropped by over 8% over the past two months and now yields close to 11%, while expecting to have its distribution coverage ratio improve from ~1.1x to ~1.3x on a distributable cash flow basis by the middle of this year as some of its growth projects come online.

This sell-off is ironic given that the MLP sector has actually performed fairly well in recent months. As a result, the selloff was likely due to market concerns about how DKL's controlling unitholder - Delek US Holdings (DK) - will pursue the deconsolidation of its DKL position, potentially at the expense of DKL unitholders. This is especially true given that its distribution coverage has gotten fairly tight recently. However, as already stated, DKL's payout ratio is expected to improve moving forward, and DK and DKL just announced that they will be advancing the deconsolidation via a buyback program between DK and DKL in which DKL will be repurchasing its units from DK and canceling them. This should help both entities, which should help to mitigate this risk.

Additionally, its leverage ratio is currently just above 4x, but management expects to reduce it to 3.5x in the near future as EBITDA increases from new growth projects and retained cash flow is allocated toward further debt reduction.

With a quality portfolio of assets concentrated in the prolific Permian Basin, an improving distribution coverage ratio, and a 48.25-quarter streak of distribution growth, DKL's 11% yield and inflation-beating growth prospects make it a highly compelling buy on the dip. This is especially notable in contrast to the broader midstream space, which has surged over the past year, while DKL has been largely overlooked by the market.

Buy The Dip Opportunity #3

Another compelling opportunity is NNN REIT (NNN), a blue-chip triple-net lease REIT with a phenomenal track record of growing its dividend year after year and generating very stable cash flows through all kinds of economic environments, including the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 crash.

On top of that, NNN pays an attractive 5.6% dividend yield and currently trades at a 2% discount to its net asset value—despite trading as high as a 13% premium to NAV back in October of last year—indicating that it could have significant valuation and multiple expansion potential in the near future.

The company is also expected to generate an inflation-beating 4.1% dividend per share CAGR over the next several years:

When you combine the 5.6% dividend yield, low risk profile, valuation multiple expansion potential, and analysts' expectations of a 4.1% dividend CAGR over the next several years, NNN REIT looks like a very attractive risk-adjusted investment after its recent 15% stock price dip.

The main risk moving forward is that long-term interest rates will continue to rise, thereby weighing on NNN's stock price, increasing its cost of capital, and ultimately slowing its growth. However, given that NNN's P/NAV valuation is discounted to its historical averages, it has a nice margin of safety that makes it an attractive risk-adjusted buying opportunity for investors with diversified portfolios.

Buy The Dip Opportunity #4

A fourth buy-the-dip opportunity right now is Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC). It has declined by over 23% since last October due to concerns about rising interest rates and anti-wind power rhetoric from the Trump administration, yet it continues to generate very strong results.

For example, BEP recently announced 10% year-over-year FFO per unit growth for 2024, along with a 5% distribution per unit hike. It also holds a BBB+ credit rating, has well-laddered debt maturities, and benefits from highly contracted cash flows, with 70% of them indexed to inflation. Additionally, its growth outlook remains impressive, thanks to its exposure to AI-driven tailwinds, making BEP a highly compelling opportunity right now.

In particular, BEP’s total return potential looks very attractive, as analysts expect its AFFO per unit to grow at an 11.7% CAGR through 2029.

When you combine this with its 6.5% distribution yield, BEP appears positioned to deliver mid-to-high-teens annualized total returns even without accounting for potential valuation multiple expansion. However, that valuation multiple expansion potential is also very real, given that it currently trades at just 12.6 times its AFFO, despite its five-year average price-to-AFFO multiple being 20.8 times.

Last but not least, the market seems to misunderstand that BEP actually has relatively little exposure to U.S. wind power as wind power is only 20% of its total cash flow, and nearly half of its cash flow comes from outside of the United States. As a result, BEP may be the best single buy-the-dip opportunity available right now.

Investor Takeaway

As this article discusses, I love it when high-quality, big dividend growth stocks go on sale. Between SCHD, DKL, NNN, and BEP, investors have a plethora of opportunities before them right now.

With these buy-the-dip opportunities and many more, it is a very exciting time to be buying at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Strategy (MicroStrategy): Collapses With Bitcoin Drop, Still Grossly Overvalued
data.description:
I made a bearish call against Strategy (MicroStrategy) (NASDAQ:MSTR) on December 15, 2024, as shown below, since then, the stock has plummeted 40%. Some investors are always tempted to buy a market darling after a pullback, hoping momentum carries it forward again, I continue to reiterate my "Strong Sell" rating for this stock and discuss my reasons in this article.

1. Quick recap and update of previous article's bearish reason

In my previous piece, I argued that MSTR was an inefficient way to invest in Bitcoin. MSTR claims it is providing levered exposure to Bitcoin but if one wanted leveraged exposure (not that I am suggesting any investor do so), Bitcoin futures would be far more efficient than MSTR. When my previous piece came out, Bitcoin was about $104k/coin and has since declined to $84k/coin, about 20% decline, while MSTR has declined by almost 40%. In fact, if we compare against MSTR's peak of $543/share on November 20, 2024, MSTR has declined by 55% in a little over one month despite Bitcoin prices falling about 10%+ (Bitcoin was $95k on November 20, 2024).

Declining 40-55% in two to three months despite much smaller fluctuations in the underlying asset is mind-boggling and further supports my view that even if an investor were very bullish on Bitcoin, MSTR is a very inefficient and risky way to gain exposure.

2. MSTR is still way overvalued

If we take a closer look, MSTR currently holds around 500k Bitcoins, which is worth roughly $42.2 billion at latest market prices of $84.5k/coin.

MSTR has roughly $7 billion debt on Dec-24 balance sheet. Let's say Bitcoin is relatively flat for next 5 years, then this debt will have to be paid, leaving MSTR shareholders with $35 billion of net assets. The market cap is currently $66 billion which is of almost double net assets - a premium of around $31 billion (90%). So anyone buying into MSTR needs Bitcoin to double just to breakeven over the long run at the current $255/share (those investors that bought in the $300s/$400s/$500/share would need Bitcoin to go up even more to breakeven).

The above assumes MSTR's underlying software business has 0 value (which is quite generous given that it has been oscillating between loss-making and slightly profitable for the past decade.

3. MSTR's past financing accomplishments may not be reflective of future market conditions

Historically, MSTR has been able to issue bonds at very low-interest rates and this has been one of MSTR's selling propositions. Indeed, this is why many investors are bullish on the stock. But there's a glitch.

A: MSTR is not getting money for free despite low stated interest rates. Below is a summary of MSTR's bond issuances from its Dec-24 SEC filing. While the stated interest rates are low, the initial conversion price (or strike price) by now is below market price. Were these convertible noteholders to convert to equity now (which they cannot yet as they have not reached the earliest conversion date), then it would dilute existing shareholders. To take the example of the 2028 Convertible Notes of $1.01 billion issued in September, were these converted to equity at $183.19/share, this would increase shares outstanding by 5 million (roughly 2% of outstanding shares). So looking at the stated interest rate is only one side of the coin - convertible note holders are not providing this money for free, they are getting something in return.

B. Now, even if we take a leap of faith and believe that MSTR is somehow profiting from convertible note holders' eagerness to get exposure to Bitcoin. Some commentators have suggested that MSTR is actually in the business of selling volatility.

To justify the premium between its market cap and net assets, MSTR would need to issue bonds on a gargantuan scale to capture $31 billion for its existing shareholders. And that would only just make current shareholders whole.

Let's do a rough estimate - given 10 year treasury yields are at 4.2%, let's assume a company like MicroStrategy would raise money on the open market for 8% interest a year. A 5 year period is 40% cumulative interest (without compounding). Let's assume MicroStrategy is able to issue these bonds at 0% interest with potential conversion rights at strike prices above market price (i.e. out of the money) and the stock price does not reach the strike price, then basically MicroStrategy got to use the proceeds for free. In order to gain $30 billion of value, MicroStrategy would need to issue $75 billion of bonds at these favorable conditions with these favorable results.

So far, MSTR has only issued a billion or two of convertible notes here and there. Maybe there's the odd buyer who finds these interesting additions to their portfolio, and maybe it's really a good deal for MSTR. But can it be replicated up to the scale of $75 billion at these terms? I'm skeptical. When markets are giddy, it always sounds easy, but when the market hits a snag, like recently with market favorites such as Tesla (TSLA) seeing their stock prices fall from $490/share to $292/share in 2 months, the bullish animal spirits vanish and there is less demand for 0% convertible notes to invest in a company that goes all-in Bitcoin. Three months ago, that may have sounded exciting to certain investors, but once risk appetite vanishes, it sounds well, a lot less tantalizing.

C. MSTR's recent issuance has performed poorly. MSTR's recent issuance in November of $2.6 billion convertible notes with a $660+ strike and having its stock price halve to $255, I would surmise that the next lender would not be so generous on its terms with MSTR, and if the next lender charges MSTR "market rates" of either 8% interest or a substantially lower strike price for conversion, then MSTR is unable to transfer value from bondholders to shareholders by selling volatility, then I don't see how MSTR will be able to justify its premium to net assets, much less grow its market cap sustainably.

In short, hoping that bondholders will be able to provide unlimited amounts of free money just to give investors in their portfolio "optionality" of bitcoin exposure is not a business model. Even if MSTR cinches a deal or two at $1 billion or $2 billion (and recent deals have been poorly performing), I highly doubt this can be replicated on a $75 billion+ level (which is what the current stock price is implying).

4. Bitcoin sentiment indicates Bitcoin prices may be in for a rough patch

My recent piece on an indicator of Bitcoin sentiment suggests that Bitcoin might be in for a rough patch. As discussed in the above paragraph, MSTR requires almost perfect conditions (in terms of financing and Bitcoin price appreciation) to even start justifying its market cap premium to net assets. Were market enthusiasm for Bitcoin to dampen or Bitcoin prices to range sidewards for a couple of months, MSTR's stock price could drop dramatically as it may become priced closer to net asset value rather than expectations of raising huge amounts at near nil interest rates and ploughing it into soaring Bitcoin prices. Since my previous piece, Bitcoin prices have slumped as they have often done when sentiment is jiggy.

5. Sum of the above

MSTR is trading at a substantial premium to net asset value and would need either (i) massive Bitcoin price gains or (ii) massive value transfer from issuing bonds at favorable terms to justify. If either does not materialize, then even if Bitcoin prices remain at current levels (and that itself is a big if, given Bitcoin's volatile historical record), MSTR has substantial downside risk.

Risks to bearish thesis

Now it could be possible (though I would argue very improbable) that MSTR issues massive amounts of bonds at favorable conditions. Who knows, there could be the off-chance that the Fed massively lowers interest rates and MSTR is able to raise hundreds of billions of dollars at minimal interest to plough into Bitcoin and keep the gravy train going for its shareholders.

Conclusion:

Avoid MSTR and buy Bitcoin directly if an investor is bullishly inclined. Ironically Michael Saylor compared Bitcoin to Manhattan real estate, and even if this is true, MSTR rather reminds me of the CDO squared subprime debt in the 2008 financial crisis. While the underlying asset (house prices in America) eventually came back and hit record highs, the CDO squared holders were wiped out in the liquidity crunch. CDOs are the cautionary tale of leverage and opacity: Too much leverage packed into an opaque structure assuming too many things need to operate perfectly may not survive a downturn even if the underlying asset eventually recovers and does well.

data.title: Aperam: Universal Acquisition Will Jump-Start EBITDA Result
data.description:
Introduction

In a previous article, I argued that I liked to pick up cyclical stocks during a downcycle. This sometimes meant I bought too soon, and I'm still seeing the share price continue to decline. In some cases, the negative impact is mitigated by a generous dividend, and that’s indeed the case with Aperam (OTC:APEMY) (OTCPK:APMSF), which continued to pay a quarterly dividend of 0.50 EUR per quarter despite the weaker stainless steel market.

Aperam has its main listing on Euronext Amsterdam, where the stock is trading with APAM as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume is approximately 180,000 shares, making it the most liquid listing to use for trading purposes. There are approximately 72.3M shares outstanding, resulting in a market cap of approximately 2.2B EUR. I will use the Euro as the base currency throughout this article, unless indicated otherwise.

Persistent weakness, but there should be light at the end of the tunnel

In the final quarter of 2024, Aperam reported a total revenue of 1.47B EUR, on which it generated an adjusted EBITDA of 116M EUR, representing an adjusted EBITDA margin of approximately 7.9%. This represents a QoQ increase, while it also represents an increase of more than 100% compared to the EBITDA margin in the final quarter of 2023.

The reported EBITDA was even higher due to the positive impact of non-recurring items to the tune of 2M EUR in the quarter. That’s good news, but this obviously doesn’t mean Aperam is out of the woods yet. The company reported a pre-tax income of 58M EUR and a net profit of just 12M EUR, which represents an EPS of 17 cents per share.

The average tax rate of 79% obviously is not representative, as it included some non-recurring items related to deferred tax assets and derecognized tax losses. As the cash flow statement below shows, the company paid just 4M EUR in cash taxes. The operating cash flow was approximately 172M EUR, but this included a 133M EUR contribution from changes in the working capital elements, which means the underlying operating cash flow was just 39M EUR and approximately 35M EUR after also taking the lease payments into consideration.

The total capex was just 27M EUR, which means Aperam was still free cash flow positive, but it's clear the free cash flow result was pretty marginal and the dividend was not fully covered. The full-year results paint a similar picture: The adjusted operating cash flow was approximately 220M EUR, which was definitely sufficient to cover the 152M EUR in capital expenditures, but it clearly fell short from covering the 145M EUR cash dividend.

While it’s nice to see Aperam is appeasing its income-focused shareholders by keeping the dividend unchanged, it's clear that although the situation is improving compared to 2023, it’s perhaps not the best idea to continue the very generous dividend payments.

That being said, the 152M EUR in capex and 155M EUR in total capex also takes 54M EUR in growth investments into account, as shown above. This means the sustaining free cash flow in FY 2024 was actually 120M EUR, and that does almost cover the dividend payments.

The acquisition of Universal

Aperam continues to expand and to diversify its business. The company announced in October it is planning to acquire Universal Stainless & Alloy Products (USAP) for $45 per share in cash, and this transaction was completed in January. This acquisition will provide Aperam with a stronger position in the aerospace segment, as that represents about 82% of Universal’s revenue.

The acquisition was completed at an anticipated EBITDA multiple of just under 7 using the consensus estimates for 2025, and on top of that, Aperam expects to unlock 27M EUR in synergy benefits by Y5, of which about a third will be unlocked by the end of this year.

Based on these elements, I think the USAP acquisition could prove to be very valuable to Aperam going forward, as the revenue is stickier than its core business in the stainless steel sector.

Investment thesis

Aperam’s share price is up by almost 20% since my previous article was published, and although I definitely still like the company, it looks like the expectations for 2025 are already built into the share price. That being said, looking at the longer-term future, I expect the company’s EBITDA to grow pretty substantially again from 2026 on (the 2025 reported EBITDA will obviously be positively impacted by the USAP EBITDA that will be consolidated from this year on). Looking at the consensus estimates for 2026, the anticipation is for Aperam to report a 655M EUR EBITDA.

Even if I’d use a 10% lower number, the EPS will still exceed 3.15 EUR per share, and that makes Aperam still attractive, not in the least because the dividend yield is still approximately 6.6-6.7% and that’s a generous compensation while waiting for a cyclical improvement.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Crescent Energy: A Joint Venture And Earnings Announcement
data.description:
Crescent Energy (NYSE:CRGY) announced the fourth quarter and annual earnings. Tucked in with that announcement is a joint venture in the Uinta Basin. Probably overshadowing the earnings report is the growth in cash flow. The management of this firm has considerable experience building and selling companies. Therefore, cash flow and free cash flow will be a very high priority in the process of building this company. This will also be the year in which focusing on the Eagle Ford is a strategy without a doubt.

Last Article

The last article announced yet another Eagle Ford acquisition. Clearly, the company intends to build a commanding and very profitable position in the Eagle Ford.

The growth occurred with both oily acreage and gassy acreage. The Eagle Ford has a transportation advantage in that the basin is closer to much of the exporting facilities that are coming online.

Management did state that the SilverBow acquisition was doing better than expected. However, what will likely prove to be more significant is the well savings and increasing performance shown above because there is a whole lot of money to be made from that situation.

Those kinds of results are likely what is powering the acquisition strategy in the Eagle Ford. Management sees a bunch of "unnatural" operators whose acreage can be run far better by the company. The improvements shown could make the bargain purchases a far better bargain in the long term.

Uinta Joint Venture

Additionally, management announced a joint venture in the Uinta basin, where the company also has acreage.

This is a basin where technology has changed the profit metrics by providing more free cash flow than was the case a few years back. Nonetheless, with management concentrating on the Eagle Ford, the company brought on a joint venture partner to help out with the development of this acreage.

As can be seen above, there are plenty of intervals yet to be exploited profitably. A decent partner can help with that.

The Uinta has been on the "back burner" compared to the Eagle Ford acquisitions (and activity in general). This type of result may allow this acreage to better compete for more capital in the future.

The joint venture is developing the Eastern acreage, while management deals with the Western acreage. It will be interesting to see how the future unfolds if these results hold up. The acreage holds a lot of possibilities from all the intervals: but only if the acreage intervals can compete for capital with the Eagle Ford acreage.

Finances

KKR as managers of this company have made no secret since the company went public that they intend for the debt to become investment grade. There are some serious interest cost savings that go with that goal. KKR likely had more experience than most managements, achieving investment grade in their ventures.

Despite all of the acquisitions, the debt leverage ratio remains within acceptable limits. This KKR venture is unusual in that low debt ratios have been a characteristic of the company ever since it became a public entity.

What is going to help the debt ratio considerably is the well cost reduction and performance increase. Those results will affect EBITDA, which is the numerator of the debt ratio. Increasing EBITDA means that less debt repayment is needed to meet debt ratio goals in the future. The company can also get an investment grade rating faster by working on both the numerator and the denominator of the debt ratio calculation.

Cash Flow

The big news here is that free cash flow as defined by this company roughly doubled.

Note also that adjusted EBTIDAX is up nicely as well. These are things that this management is focused upon growing.

When this many significant acquisitions are made in one year, then the income statement is probably next to useless. But cash flow often shows the operational progress made.

Operational Costs

Additionally, management broke out the operational costs so that investors had some idea as to the progress made (and also where the new fiscal year would begin).

Note that the December operating expense per unit is the lowest of the figures. That bodes very well for the first quarter of the coming fiscal year.

General and administrative expenses are also declining on a per unit basis as well. Evidently, this management planned for growth early on. So, the administrative resources were already in place when that growth occurred. This enabled the company to realize some economies of scale administratively.

Summary

This company is in a position to realize much lower average expenses in the coming fiscal year, with likely more cost progress coming.

The company also has considerable natural gas exposure from the SilverBow acquisition, in time to benefit from the expected natural gas pricing recovery. The very cold winter is likely to help the first quarter report considerably when it comes to natural gas prices.

This company remains a strong buy consideration. KKR is making solid progress building a very profitable core Eagle Ford position. Free cash flow as defined by the company increased nicely in the latest fiscal year despite the material acquisitions. Investors can likely expect another big jump in free cash flow in the coming year.

Now, when it comes to 2025 guidance, that guidance is only good until the next acquisition is made. For that reason, I would not spend a lot of time on it.

Rarely do investors get to invest alongside an organization with the stature of KKR. This venture appears to be going a long way to adding to their reputation.

Risks

Fast growth has its own risk. KKR has a lot of experience piecing together a successful company and selling that. But there is no guarantee that this venture and its strategies will be successful.

Any upstream company is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn of those prices could materially change the company's outlook.

The loss of key personnel is less likely to be an issue with KKR management. The organization itself is much larger than the company and therefore has access to a lot of talent on short notice.

data.title: Ulta Beauty: Getting More Attractive With Each Visit, Just Like Its Customers
data.description:
Ulta Beauty, Inc. (ULTA) is an omnichannel specialty retailer, engaged primarily in the sale of cosmetics and accessories, that boasts a strong domestic presence of over 1,400 locations and a robust ecommerce presence (20% of revenue based on my calculations). Founded in Lombard, IL in 1990, ULTA now has a market cap of $16.9 billion and over $11 billion in annual revenue. Despite operating in a competitive and what is often-considered by Wall Street to be a highly discretionary industry, ULTA has demonstrated resilient operating performance with an "Amazon-proof" business model. Coupled with shareholder-friendly capital allocation, ULTA is in my estimation a misunderstood high-quality company trading at an attractive price with multiple value-driving levers.

Given the competitive and sometimes cyclical nature of retail, one might expect greater volatility in the operating performance of a retailer relative to the market. On the contrary, ULTA has delivered remarkably consistent operating margin when you exclude the obvious one-off 2020 to 2021 COVID-related lockdown-induced dip.

One might wonder how a retailer like ULTA who sells what are believed to be discretionary goods did during the Global Financial Crisis. Despite one's expectations, ULTA has been profitable every year for the past 20 years. EPS grew in 2006, 2007, and 2008. It dipped slightly (~10%) in 2009 and saw rapid growth of over 50% in 2010. The worst year for EPS was due to COVID-related lockdowns. Afterwards, the EPS resumed its upward trajectory. I don't put any weight into the estimate (greyed righthand section) on the chart below, but it is there for reference from an independent source.

ULTA's organic growth, through increasing store count and driving same-store sales (through increasing ticket size and transaction volume), has enabled this magnificent compounding we've witnessed. Given the rise of Sephora (with its 40 million loyalty members and its near-term presence in every Kohl's location), investors are left wondering if ULTA can keep compounding at attractive rates. I rebut this with the following considerations:

  • First, ULTA is increasing its penetration in Target stores, which should drive incremental volumes and increase brand awareness.
  • Second, ULTA is entering the Mexican market by partnering with Grupo Axo. International growth is not something ULTA has previously embarked on. If successful, international expansion offers significant (and not-priced-in) growth potential.
  • Third, ULTA is increasing its bond with its loyal customers (ULTA has 44.4 million loyalty members; Sally Beauty has only 16 million for comparison) by delivering strong in-person connections to brands. Importantly, the recent announcement of integrating Cécred (Beyoncé Knowles' backed salons) across its store footprint highlights a significantly differentiating value proposition, one that no other cosmetic retailer offers.

Beyond the growth potential, ULTA is attractive for two other critical reasons. The first is that the company has been generously rewarding shareholders with significant buybacks and plans to continue to do so aggressively. This suggests not only positive alignment between the management team and shareholders, but it has the potential to be substantially value accretive when considering this next point.

Valuation is important. We've already determined that what we're looking at is a high-quality, resilient specialty retailer, one that boasts consistent margins and has developed a moat within its niche. The next logical question is what are we potentially paying for these assets. Well, based on the following table, a buyer today would be paying the lowest price-to-sales, price-to-earnings, price-to-forward earnings, price-to-cash flow, and price-to-book of any of the calendar years shown. Buying back shares at these low multiples should be greatly accretive to shareholders.

While the thesis for ULTA appears to be strong, we must understand the risks to the thesis. The biggest risk is a slowdown in ULTA sales due to competitive pressures. While I believe ULTA has a strong moat, this is a possibility and the negative impact on earnings growth would feed into the valuation. Second, ULTA is unproven internationally. While the entrance into foreign markets increases the total addressable market, it also may increase execution risk. Mismanaging these risks could prove costly. This is somewhat mitigated by ULTA's partnering with a local group, rather than attempting greenfield expansion alone. Lastly, inflation may remain sticky, interest rates may remain high, and unemployment has been ticking up. ULTA is a consumer-centric business and the macroeconomic environment influences discretionary spending (including cosmetics). Reduced discretionary income from these factors could weigh on category spending. A recession could also strongly dampen ULTA's near-term prospects.

In this article, we explored misconceptions Wall Street has about ULTA. While cosmetics are seemingly discretionary, just ask your teenage daughter how discretionary they really are. ULTA is profitable through downturns with remarkable resiliency. In addition to a great business, management has shown a propensity to buy back shares and intends to do so aggressively. Another consideration is that in a high interest-rate environment and one with uncertainty, ULTA boasts no long-term debt. There you have it. ULTA is a high-quality, growing business with a clean balance sheet and shareholder-friendly management, trading at a steep discount to historical valuation metrics. I don't know about you, but I like what I see.

data.title: FSCO: Discount Narrowing But The Fund Could Deserve A Premium
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

FS Credit Opportunities Corp. (NYSE:FSCO) is a closed-end fund with an inception date that goes back to December 2013. However, it only became a publicly listed CEF in November 2022, spending much of its existence as a private fund. They focus on senior loan exposure but are a bit unique from their other publicly traded CEF senior loan peers, with their private credit exposure comprising nearly 60% of the fund's invested capital.

Since hitting the public stage, FSCO has delivered strong results, outpacing a wide variety of other senior loan-focused peers. Even despite the shallow discount that FSCO is trading at now relative to where it was, FSCO could be a solid choice for investors.

FSCO Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 1.52
  • Discount/Premium: -4.01%
  • Distribution Yield: 11.17%
  • Expense Ratio: 4.35%
  • Leverage: 32.22%
  • Managed Assets: $2.119 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

FSCO's investment objective is "to generate an attractive total return consisting of a high level of current income and capital appreciation, with a secondary objective of capital preservation."

This is attempted to be achieved with these key points:

• Diversified credit strategy investing across public and private credit in loans, bonds, structured credit or highly structured equity investments and across fixed and floating rate assets.

• Focus on investments where return premiums exist due to complexity of company balance sheets, illiquidity of the asset or resulting from corporate events.

The fund utilizes leverage, which can help to provide potentially enhanced returns but also creates increased volatility and risk. Further, it increases the expenses of the fund, and the total expense ratio comes in at 7.60%. A portion of the fund's leverage is through borrowings that come with a floating rate based on SOFR plus 2.65%. However, they are primarily leveraged through fixed-rate preferred shares. That sets the fund up to benefit significantly from a higher rate environment but could also hurt the spread now that the Fed has cut rates by 100 basis points.

FSCO is quite a large fund as well, providing plenty of liquidity. The portfolio is invested in 76 companies, while floating rate assets comprise 63% of the fund. 82% of the fund is invested in senior-secured debt. That's split between 66% first lien, 4% second lien and 12% senior secured bonds. As can be expected, the fund's duration is very low based on the floating rate exposure of the fund.

FSCO Vs. Peers

One of my initial concerns would have been the fund's incentive fees, which is one of the reasons why the fund's expense ratio is so high. This is primarily something that is more common in private or more complex investment structures. Which, to be fair, FSCO is more privately tilted, so it can make sense.

Under the A&R Investment Advisory Agreement, the incentive fee is calculated and payable quarterly in arrears based upon the Fund's "pre-incentive fee net investment income" for the immediately preceding quarter, and is subject to a preferred return rate, expressed as a rate of return on the Fund's net assets, equal to 1.50% per quarter (or an annualized hurdle rate of 6.00%), subject to a "catch-up" feature.

Incentive fees aren't necessarily automatically negative as they can also align management with the shareholders. That said, Incentive fees add an additional layer of expenses. For FSCO, the hurdle rate is quite low, and that can be a bit concerning.

Investors will more commonly be familiar with them in business development companies ("BDCs") or the collateralized loan obligation CEFs. This is a question they touched on in their latest earnings call as well:

Joseph Montelione

Next question. Can you discuss your fee structure relative to your peers in the closed end fund space?

Andrew Beckman

Yes, this is a question we get a lot because we have an incentive fee and we also have a management fee that can optically be viewed as being a tad higher relative to kind of closed end fund peers. I think the thing that's important is we view our peers as not just closed end funds, but a mixture of closed end funds as well as BDCs. If you remember from my remarks, 58% of our fund is invested in private credit. So not only is that BDC like if you look at our average private investment, it is a more tailored, more structured and more intensive private investment than the average investment the BDC makes. But like if you wanted to simplify things and say 58% of our fund is like the BDC and 42% of our fund is like the closed end fund and blended the fees, you would see something that really justifies our fees and maybe even makes our fees look a bit low.

However, at the end of the day, it isn't about what the expenses are necessarily but what the fund can deliver. On that front, we have seen quite incredible results.

Since the fund's inception, it has easily outpaced its peers on a total share price return basis. On a total NAV return basis, they have also continued to outperform, though not to the same degree. This outperformance is against a wide variety of other CEF peers, as well as the passively managed Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN). BKLN can act as a sort of benchmark for the senior loan investment space. The only fund to underperform BKLN during this period was First Trust Senior Floating Rate Income Fund II (FCT). However, it wasn't to a large degree.

In this case, we can clearly see that the higher expense ratio could have been justified.

Even against some of the other more eccentric peers, FSCO has delivered quite respectable results. We can refer to this group as "cousin funds" in that they share some overlap and similarities but enough differences to not be exact peers.

That can include the hybrid exposure of senior loans and CLOs such as XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT) and Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC); it can also include the equity CLO funds Oxford Lane Capital Corp (OXLC) and Eagle Point Credit Company (ECC)-though ultimately falling shy of their results, FSCO had performed fairly respectably on a total NAV return basis nonetheless.

On a total share price return basis, we see once again that they came out on top by a wide margin.

The leverage these funds use can help explain this outperformance during a period of time that has worked out incredibly well for the space. They invest in junk, but defaults have been quite minimal and even when there are defaults and bankruptcies, recovery from senior loans is relatively strong historically.

Of course, that isn't always the case and senior loans are more heavily impacted by interest rates. In this last round of the Fed raising rates, the recovery rates between the two started to converge.

With rates coming back down as the Fed cut 100 basis points, that is easing some of the pressure. Though rates are still off the zero interest rate level, and aren't expecting to go back down to that level either, leaving more attractive yields for investors.

Discount Narrowing Drives Results

Going back to the outperformance of FSCO, we know that the fund saw its total share price return significantly outpace its peers but also its own total NAV return level. For most CEF investors, they know that comes from the result of a fund's discount narrowing. FSCO has seen its discount narrow quite materially. In fact, at one point, this fund dropped to a nearly 35% discount.

Certainly, investors taking a position in this fund at that time or really even at any point in the last year were richly rewarded. This is one of the primary driving factors we like to highlight in CEFs and how to exploit these sorts of opportunities.

With that, one might imagine that FSCO is no longer a deal-well, I believe that it certainly may be no bargain today relative to where it was, but there is a real chance it could trade at a premium. Premium funds tend to trade at a premium. If they can keep up such strong performance relative to peers and an elevated distribution yield, a premium valuation may not be that unexpected.

Even its peers in the senior loan space, which we highlighted above, trade at premiums or near premiums, with FSCO actually appearing to be trading at the widest discount.

The incentive fee could have or be keeping investors away from the fund, or it being relatively newer to the public space could also be something investors could be cautious about. That said, as the discount has narrowed rapidly, it appears more investors are starting to take notice.

Similarly, we see that even its not-so-peer cousin funds are trading at more elevated levels, often regularly at premiums. OXLC and ECC only provide monthly NAV data, but we can still get the general idea.

Strong Distribution Yield

Another reason the fund could be garnering more interest is that they recently raised their distribution. This was after funds like XFLT, VVR and EFR cut their distribution, ECC stopped paying their supplemental and BKLN's monthly dividend has also been declining. The latest monthly distribution comes in at $0.0645. Against the Q3 net investment income of $0.21, that works out to 108.5% NII coverage.

As rates are cut by the Fed, that is going to negatively impact income generation in these funds, so that's why we've seen cuts. So, for FSCO to be going in the opposite direction, that is likely earning it some notice. That said, Q3's number doesn't yet reflect the rate cuts at this point. Even with the Q4 results on deck soon, we won't see the actual full 100 basis point of cut impact until Q1 2025.

For some greater context, we can see that the NAV rate for FSCO comes in at 10.69%. That can be compared to VVR having a NAV distribution rate that currently works out to 11.69%, FRA at 11.57% and FCT at 10.86%. The funds with lower NAV distribution rates come from BSL at an 8.79% rate and EFR at 8.48%. When compared to its cousin funds, XFLT has a NAV distribution rate of 14.09%, and ECC has a NAV rate of 20.74%.

Conclusion

FSCO hit the public market and dropped to a massive discount. Since then, the discount has narrowed materially. The fund comes with an incentive fee on top of its management fee, adding a fee layer. However, it is a bit more of a complexly managed fund with its private investment exposure. That can make it more like a hybrid of a BDC. Further, the results of the fund have also been able to deliver significantly higher total NAV returns, meaning that they have otherwise been earning and justifying that fee structure.

While it certainly is no bargain as it was when we saw a double-digit discount, relative to a handful of its peers and even the more eccentric cousin funds available, it is trading at a discounted level. In my opinion, I could see this fund being another CEF that could get to the premium level and trade there regularly at some point.

data.title: FEMSA: Q4 Rebound In Proximity America's Growth Is Encouraging - Maintain Buy Rating
data.description:
I initiated coverage on Fomento Económico Mexicano, S.A.B. de C.V. (NYSE:FMX) in early December after returning from a vacation in Mexico City. The company, more commonly known as FEMSA, operates a conglomerate of businesses ranging from soda bottling, to convenience store operations, to retail pharmacies. The company is based in Mexico City, but some of its segments operate in (or sell to) other select Latin American countries.

FEMSA seemed thoroughly unloved when I initiated my Buy rating about three months ago, and my article was titled "FEMSA Looks Like A Terrific Contrarian Pick In Times Of Fear For Mexico". As that title implies, the company's collection of businesses weren't (and aren't) absent of risks, but I felt that the market was overplaying those risks without more closely examining them.

After hitting a low of ~$81 following my initial investment recommendation, FMX shares have performed a U-Turn, putting me in a profitable position. The recently reported Q4 results add support for the bullish view. I maintain a Buy rating.

Brief Recap of FEMSA's Business Interests

FEMSA has five main business lines:

  1. The company's ~50% ownership, but greater corporate control of, Coca-Cola FEMSA (KOF), a Latin-American bottler of soda products, juices and other non-carbonated drinks.
  2. OXXO, a large Mexican-based convenience store chain that competes with international stores like 7-Eleven and Circle-K. This segment is called "Proximity Americas".
  3. A similar, but small, convenience store chain operating in Europe (called "Proximity Europe").
  4. A Health Division that operates pharmacies in Mexico and other Latin-American countries.
  5. A Fuel Services business that runs gas stations next to some OXXO outlets. The fuel services is a small segment (~10%) with low margins, and is not a significant driver of the business.

Nearly 90% of segment EBITDA comes from OXXO and the company's share of Coca-Cola FEMSA.

Meanwhile, Proximity Europe is the fastest growing segment for the company.

Investor Concerns / Reasons From Stock Weakness

While the election of Donald Trump for a second term as U.S. President was undoubtedly bad news for Mexico as a whole, FMX shares had been retreating well before that.

In my view, there were several reasons for the weakness, including:

  • Slowing growth for Proximity Americas
  • A weakening Mexican Peso (MXN:USD)
  • Investor concerns about FEMSA's changing business focus towards a conglomerate of varied interests and exposures

The election of Donald Trump certainly didn't help sentiment, and FMX shares floundered into the new year amid trade tariff concerns, a continued strong USD, likely along with some tax-loss selling.

My inspection of FEMSA's businesses, however, didn't yield much evident exposure to potential tariffs. Coca-Cola FEMSA distributes products in Mexico and some Central American and South American regions, but it would make no sense for the company to export to the United States (where Coca-Cola has its own bottling operations). Meanwhile, OXXO wouldn't be expected to ship product to the United States, but could have some minor exposure to potential retaliatory tariffs impacting American products on its convenience store shelves.

I concluded that the extended weakness in FMX stock was more attributable to sentiment than any legitimate incremental business concerns. With no immediate positive catalysts to reverse the trend, shares continued to slowly drop.

The latest quarterly results were just what the doctor ordered.

Q4 2024 FEMSA Results

The most reassuring thing coming from FEMSA's Q4 2024 results was the fact that Proximity Americas (OXXO) growth re-emerged after a couple of weak quarters. As I mentioned in November, management had attributed the prior growth slowdown to weather and calendar issues, and I gave management the benefit of the doubt on those attributions. OXXO revenues rose 13.2% year-over-year for Q4. The fact that these gains were against strong Q4 2023 comps adds credence to the growth story. The rebound is attributable to both higher average purchases and a gross margin increase of more than 200 bps.

Total revenues rose 12.8% (in MXN terms), and in organic terms that growth was 10.8%. Those results beat Wall Street consensus estimates by about 4%. EBITDA rose more than 25% to about $710 million. I'm using a USD/MXN rate of 20.5 for all translations in this article.

On a per-ADS basis, adjusted net income came in just under $1.50 for the quarter. Seeking Alpha suggests the Wall Street consensus was $1.65, but I believe that translated into higher full-year 2024 earnings than the Street was looking for. I believe part of the confusion here is that many more analysts have annual estimates for FEMSA than quarterly estimates. I think it's clear that Q4 surpassed expectations, and the 5% rise in FMX on February 27 reflects that.

FEMSA Outlook

I found a few interesting takeaways from FEMSA's results and conference call. First of all, there was no mention of tariffs neither in management's opening comments nor the healthy volume of questions from analysts. That offers confirmation to me that FEMSA's businesses aren't at high exposure to tariffs, at least not directly. On a related basis, OXXO's expansion into the United States seems uninterrupted, although I do believe the company should be cautious about their expectations, given the unpredictability of U.S. President Trump.

Another observation was the mention of the word "cannibalization," which suggests that OXXO location growth is likely maxed out in some regions. That's not necessarily what an investor wants to hear, but I'm comforted that management is aware and monitoring the risk. Customer visits per location were down in the quarter (versus the higher spending per visit).

Third, FEMSA has a not-so-common strategy of striving to increase leverage. I have two views on this. First, the company will be pursuing significant shareholder returns, which is obviously a positive.

Our plans for 2025 to be presented at the next shareholders' meeting are to deploy, including ordinary dividends, almost MXN 66 billion or $3.2 billion of current exchange rates over the next 12 months. This amount includes MXN 14.8 billion of ordinary dividends and MXN 51.2 billion of extraordinary dividends and buybacks, representing a 10.4% yield for shareholders at the current market capitalization.

-Source: Q4 2024 Conference Call

While I'm not a big fan of highly leveraged companies, to be more specific, I'm cautious on already-highly-leveraged companies. Adding leverage can increase ROE and Net Income, with the offset absorbing a higher risk profile. I'm much less excited, naturally, about highly leveraged companies who are already earning those beefed-up returns on equity. So this is mostly a positive, although I do hope FEMSA management will be cautious about their corporate leverage given all the economic uncertainties right now.

The last thing I want to note from the conference call is management's cautious statement about the current operating environment:

"We begin 2025 facing an increasingly challenging consumer environment in many of our markets, particularly Mexico, but we are confident we have a compelling opportunity set in front of us, a solid strategy to pursue those opportunities, and the best team in the business,”

- José Antonio Fernandez Carbajal, CEO (Source)

In aggregate, there's a good reason for a cautious outlook for FEMSA; more than I'd expect absent those comments from the conference call. That gives me some pause, as I update my thoughts about valuation.

Valuation and Outlook

Keeping in mind the 10:1 ADS / common share ratio for FEMSA, I'm calculating a 2024 net income of about $5.66 per share. When I wrote in early-December, the analyst consensus was for $4.95. The beat isn't fully clean, as there were a number of adjustments. That helps explain why Wall Street is only looking for earnings of $5.05 per share for 2025. On that basis, FMX is trading at a forward P/E of about 18.6x.

It might be best to value FEMSA relative to EBITDA, especially as management pursues higher leverage. On that basis, I see an EV/EBITDA of about 6.8x. Although perfect comps are difficult given the company's varied interests, it's worth noting that both Alimentation Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) and Seven & i Holdings (OTCPK:SVNDY) trade at EV/EBITDAs north of 9x.

Both those convenience store comps carry higher leverage as well, giving me some comfort about FEMSA management's interest to lever up.

While management's commentary was cautious, I think that the market is already pricing in a lot of risk in FEMSA - some of that related to trade concerns. Not only do I continue to believe that any tariffs against Mexico (from the U.S.) will have limited impact on FEMSA, but I believe the prospect exists for non-U.S. countries to strengthen their trade ties with other partners. That could result in more economic cooperation amongst Latin-American countries - something that would benefit FEMSA due to its operations in places like Chile, Colombia, Peru, and Brazil.

Additionally, I'm not a U.S. Dollar bull at this stage, and believe that a lot of the country's strong-arm tactics will backfire, ultimately hurting confidence in the currency. A weakening U.S. Dollar would present a tailwind for Fomento Económico Mexicano's ADS trading on the NYSE.

I continue to rate FMX a Buy following the latest results and news.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Carillon Chartwell Real Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Inflation remains elevated

The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics’ Consumer Price Index (CPI), which attempts to reflect the change in prices for U.S. consumers across all goods and services purchased, bottomed in September and started to inflect higher during the quarter. Trailing 12-month core CPI, a measure that strips out volatile components like food and energy prices, now sits at 3.3%. There is room for this number to move lower over the first quarter as the economy starts to annualize some elevated readings from early 2024, but the fact remains that inflation is still well above the U.S. Federal Reserves’ self-mandated target of 2%.

The Fed tempers rate cut expectations

With inflation remaining stubbornly high and the economy on seemingly solid ground, Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) participants reduced their rate cut expectations for 2025. In the September survey of the FOMC, the median expectation was for 100 basis points (BPs) of cuts for the following calendar year. The most recent survey, taken in December, saw the median expectation drop to just 50 bps in cuts for 2025. The move to tighten the outlook for financial conditions strengthened the value of the dollar against many foreign currencies and sent short-term U.S. Treasury rates higher.

Rates move higher

It wasn’t just short-term rates that moved higher during the quarter. For various reasons, rates across the yield curve saw upward pressure. With the Fed’s hawkish turn, the market started to move up the terminal rate, which is the level of the overnight lending rate set by the Fed that the market believes will serve as the floor for this economic cycle. Adding to the pressures on the long end of the curve were increased worries over inflation and the government’s ability to rein in fiscal spending. While the U.S. government’s 2024 budget deficit likely shrank from the 6.3% level recorded in the prior year, it undoubtedly remains elevated relative to any other time in U.S. history outside a recession. Investors have started to question what the fiscal situation in the United States will look like when the next recession does eventually arrive.

Credit spreads at historical tights

The continued resilience in the U.S. economy combined with the attractive yields in the corporate debt market have pushed corporate credit spreads to historically tight levels. While the absolute level of credit spreads is not a reliable market timing indicator, we believe better value can be found in other asset classes like U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) and mortgages. Accordingly, we continue to rotate away from corporate credit, though we are still finding individual securities within the sector that we believe are mispriced by the market and have room to outperform.

Fund Review

Relative performance for the Fund was strong in comparison to the Morningstar Inflation-Protected Bond peer group, with the Fund’s I shares finishing in the 6th percentile out of 152 funds, as of Dec. 31, 2024. The Fund’s shorter duration and allocation to cyclical stocks helped lessen the headwind from the upward move in interest rates. While we limit the Fund’s exposure to equities to 15%, this allocation has proven to be an important factor in smoothing returns in a rising rate environment. In particular, the higher correlation between rates and cyclical equity sectors (such as energy and materials) has proven to be particularly beneficial. While equity exposure could shrink in 2025, we do believe there should remain a need to hold an allocation to stocks with commodity exposure to smooth returns.

Headwinds to performance were largely a product of duration exposure. The long end of the TIPS curve performed relatively poorly. Mortgages also produced negative returns despite attractive yields due to rising rates. Lastly, the Fund’s exposure to precious metals was a headwind to total return during the quarter.

The data are shown for informational purposes only and are not indicative of future portfolio characteristics or returns. Portfolio holdings are not stagnant and may change over time without prior notice. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Please note that the holdings identified do not represent all of the securities purchased, sold, or recommended for the fund. They are provided for informational purposes only.

Carillon Tower Advisers, Chartwell Investment Partners, their affiliates, or their respective employees may have a position in the securities listed. Please contact Carillon at 800.421.4814 to obtain the calculation’s methodology and/ or a list showing every holding’s contribution to the overall fund’s performance during the measurement period.

Top securities

EQT is the largest producer of natural gas in the Appalachian region of the United States. With rising demand from the liquified natural gas (LNG) export terminals coming online in the United States and Canada in 2025, we expect natural gas prices to be well supported in the coming quarters.

Teva Pharmaceuticals was another standout performer for the Fund. Analysts continue to be surprised by the dramatic turnaround in the company’s trajectory. Toward the end of the quarter, Teva announced favorable results from its phase 2 trial of its newest drug for Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis.

Ciena and Oracle were both beneficiaries of the exuberance behind the artificial intelligence (AI) craze. While we believe artificial intelligence will become increasingly integrated into our daily lives, we expect investors to shift their focus from individual data center and semiconductor companies toward two key areas: how these data centers will be powered, and how the massive volumes of data produced will be transported both within data centers and to end users.

The former Chesapeake Energy changed its name during the quarter to Expand Energy after the completed merger with Southwest Energy. Expand benefited from the same dynamics EQT has, namely rising gas prices but also investor excitement over future demand for LNG and power for data centers.

Bottom securities

The majority of the Fund’s bottom performers can be explained by one factor: duration. Rates across the curve moved higher into improving economic conditions and a tighter monetary policy outlook from the Fed. With longer-dated bonds more sensitive to these rate moves, they lagged dramatically. Therefore, it is no surprise that the Fund’s weakest performance came from the four TIPS with the longest duration – namely, the TIPS with stated coupon and maturity dates of 0 7/8 (0.875%) 2/15/47, 1.0% 2/15/48, 0 3/4 (0.75%) 2/15/42, and 0 5/8 (0.625%) 2/15/43.

Away from duration, the one equity holding that detracted the most from performance was Viking Therapeutics. Viking released positive topline data in a phase 1 clinical trial for its anti-obesity drug, but the results were overshadowed by the incoming administration’s announcement of its candidate to be secretary of Health and Human Services. While the nominee is likely to be approved by the U.S. Senate in the coming months, we do not believe the policies of the new administration will inhibit the potential uptake of this blockbuster drug.

Outlook

In reflecting on the year that was, it is quite impressive that the U.S. economy was able to continue to expand above the long-term growth trend, which is projected around 2%. In fact, it appears real gross domestic product (GDP) should ultimately finish 2024 closer to 3.0%, dwarfing the consensus projections of 1.0% made at the end of 2023. Growth was buoyed by consumer spending, despite wage growth cooling and consumer confidence remaining depressed.

Looking forward, we struggle to see how the economy will maintain this robust strength in the coming quarters. While it is hard to bet against the U.S. consumer, there are some warning signs such as elevated credit card and auto loan defaults starting to appear. In addition, with the Fed now pausing any further rate cuts, the hope of lower mortgage rates for potential home buyers has, at minimum, been postponed. As a result, we expect total home sales, historically a major indicator for consumer spending, to remain depressed in the coming year.

Regarding inflation, we have long been of the belief that inflation could remain structurally higher for the foreseeable future than in the post-Global Financial Crisis regime, with core CPI remaining closer to 3.0% rather than 2.0%. There are several secular reasons for this shift, including demographics, reshoring or nearshoring of manufacturing capabilities, as well as other factors. However, when it comes to the outlook over the coming quarters, we expect inflation to remain rather tame and core CPI to slowly make its way toward 2.5%.

With slowing growth and inflation reaching levels “close enough” to the Fed’s 2% mandate, we foresee the Fed resuming cuts by the spring of 2025. These cuts could exceed the 33 bps of total cuts priced into the market for the calendar year and could potentially be as high as 75 bps, assuming the Fed starts the process by March. The timing of the start of this process could determine the ultimate level of cuts as we do not see the economy remaining weak throughout the year and expect inflation to bounce back by the fourth quarter of 2025.

If the economy were to slow and the Fed were to resume rate cuts, the expectation would be for the yield curve to bull-steepen, with front-end rates dropping more than yields on longer maturities. In such an environment, risk-free rates could become more attractive than credit, and lower coupon mortgages outperform current coupon paper due to the prepayment risk found in higher coupons. While duration should perform well in this environment, we remain wary of extending the portfolio too far in what we believe could be a countertrend move lower. Inflation may go dormant for a couple of quarters, but we do believe a second wave could unfold, and with it, higher back-end rates.

In such conditions, risk assets can become volatile and, given the market’s stretched sentiment and positioning, we would expect the resilience of long-only investors to be tested. That said, liquidity remains a tailwind for the market and could potentially increase if the Fed gets more aggressive lowering rates in the summer. While we expect many stops and starts regarding the Trump administration’s goal of reauthorizing the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA), we do believe it eventually gets done and provides corporations more certainty around future tax policy, and, in turn, more confidence in upcoming capital and budget planning.

Taking all of this into account, we think it is important to remind investors that our ultimate goal for the Fund, in combination with producing current income, is achieving returns that outpace inflation over a complete market cycle. With core inflation likely around 3% and rates across the risk-free curve providing yields above this, we think it is an opportune time to buy fixed income assets like mortgages and TIPS. While the duration on the portfolio will remain well short of the Bloomberg US Treasury TIPS Index, we do feel it is an opportune time to start to lock-in yields further out the curve, preferably in the 3- to 5-year maturity range. With credit spreads tight and volatility likely picking up, we caution investors about reaching for yield until a more opportune entry point presents itself.

Risk Considerations:

Investing in any mutual fund involves risk, including the risk that you may lose all or part of the money you invest. The Fund invests in both fixed income and equity securities. Its investments in fixed income securities are subject to such risks as: interest rate risk; call risk; default risk; high yield (junk bond) risk; and unrated bond risk. The Fund also invests in equity securities that carry the potential for unpredictable drops in value and periods of lackluster performance.

Past performance is not indicative of future results, and investing involves risk, including the risk of loss. All information as of Dec. 31, 2024. Opinions expressed are the current opinions as of the date appearing in this material only. This material should not be construed as research or investment advice.
